quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 11 Quiz 4
quiz.subtitle: The Formula $V = Bh$ and Cavalieri's Principle
quiz.subject: Geometry
quiz.topic: Geometry: 11.4 Volume of Prisms and Cylinders

questions.question: Cavalieri's Principle states that two solids have the same volume if they have the same:
options: Height and cross-sectional area at every level
questions.explanation: Cavalieri's Principle: If two solids have the same height and every horizontal cross-section at the same level has the same area, then the solids have the same volume. This explains why an oblique prism and a right prism with the same base and height have equal volumes.

questions.question: A rectangular prism measures $7$ cm by $4$ cm by $9$ cm. What is its volume?
questions.explanation: $V = lwh = 7 \times 4 \times 9 = 252$ cm$^3$. Volume is measured in cubic units. The surface area would be different — $254$ cm$^2$ — but volume measures the space inside.

questions.question: A right triangular prism has a base with area $24$ in$^2$. The prism height is $11$ inches. What is its volume?
questions.explanation: $V = Bh = 24 \times 11 = 264$ in$^3$. The volume formula $V = Bh$ works for any prism — just multiply the base area by the height, regardless of the base shape.

questions.question: A cylinder has radius $5$ cm and height $12$ cm. What is its volume in terms of $\pi$?
questions.explanation: $V = \pi r^2 h = \pi(5^2)(12) = \pi(25)(12) = 300\pi$ cm$^3$. For a cylinder, the base area $B = \pi r^2$, so $V = \pi r^2 h$. This is the 3D extension of the circle area formula.

questions.question: A cylinder has diameter $8$ m and height $6$ m. What is its volume, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: Radius $= 4$ m. $V = \pi r^2 h = \pi(4^2)(6) = 96\pi \approx 301.59 \approx 301.6$ m$^3$. When given the diameter, always find the radius first before using the volume formula.

questions.question: A cylinder has volume $200\pi$ cm$^3$ and radius $5$ cm. What is its height?
questions.explanation: $V = \pi r^2 h$, so $200\pi = \pi(5^2)h = 25\pi h$. Dividing both sides by $25\pi$: $h = \frac{200\pi}{25\pi} = 8$ cm. Check: $\pi(25)(8) = 200\pi$. ✓

questions.question: A regular hexagonal prism has base side $4$ ft, apothem $3.46$ ft, and height $10$ ft. What is its volume, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: Base area $B = \frac{1}{2}aP = \frac{1}{2} \times 3.46 \times (6 \times 4) = \frac{1}{2} \times 3.46 \times 24 = 41.52$ ft$^2$. Volume $= Bh = 41.52 \times 10 = 415.2$ ft$^3$. With a decimal apothem, the volume is a decimal.

questions.question: A right rectangular prism and an oblique cylinder have the same base area of $30$ cm$^2$ and the same height of $15$ cm. According to Cavalieri's Principle, what can be said about their volumes?
options: Both have the same volume: $450$ cm$^3$
The prism has a larger volume
The cylinder has a larger volume
questions.explanation: By Cavalieri's Principle, since they have equal heights and equal cross-sectional areas at every level, their volumes are equal. $V = Bh = 30 \times 15 = 450$ cm$^3$ for both. The shape of the base doesn't matter — only the area and the height.

questions.question: A rectangular swimming pool is $25$ ft long, $12$ ft wide, and uniformly $5$ ft deep. How many cubic feet of water does it hold?
questions.explanation: $V = lwh = 25 \times 12 \times 5 = 1{,}500$ ft$^3$. This is a rectangular prism — the volume is simply length $\times$ width $\times$ depth. No $\frac{1}{2}$ or other factor is needed because the cross-section is constant.

questions.question: A cylindrical grain silo has diameter $18$ ft and height $40$ ft. What is its volume, rounded to the nearest cubic foot?
questions.explanation: Radius $= 9$ ft. $V = \pi r^2 h = \pi(9^2)(40) = \pi(81)(40) = 3{,}240\pi \approx 10{,}178.76 \approx 10{,}179$ ft$^3$. Large cylinders like silos hold enormous volumes — this one holds about $76{,}000$ gallons.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered volume formulas for prisms and cylinders. You understand $V = Bh$, can find volumes with both exact and decimal answers, apply Cavalieri's Principle, and solve real-world problems.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Remember: (1) Volume of any prism or cylinder $= Bh$, where $B$ is the base area, (2) For cylinders, $B = \pi r^2$, so $V = \pi r^2 h$, (3) Cavalieri's Principle: same height + same cross-sectional area at every level = same volume, (4) Volume is measured in cubic units.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) finding the base area first, then multiplying by height, (2) for regular polygons, use $B = \frac{1}{2}aP$, (3) when given diameter, divide by $2$ to get radius, (4) Cavalieri's Principle means an oblique solid has the same volume as a right solid with the same base and height.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! The volume formula $V = Bh$ applies to all prisms and cylinders. For a rectangular prism: $V = lwh$. For a cylinder: $V = \pi r^2 h$. For a triangular prism: $V = (\frac{1}{2}bh_{\text{triangle}}) \times h_{\text{prism}}$. Cavalieri's Principle: equal heights and equal cross-sections give equal volumes, regardless of whether the solid is right or oblique.
